concepts.title: Electrical Properties
concepts.conceptBody:
Properties Of Solids

Some of the properties of solids which are useful in electronic and magnetic devices such as, transistor, computers, and telephones etc. are summarized below:

Electrical Properties: 

Solids are classified into following classes depending on the extent of conducting nature:

Conductivity

(a) Metals : ${{10}^{8}}{{\Omega }^{-1}}~\text{c}{{\text{m}}^{-1}}$

(b) Insulators : ${{10}^{-12}}{{\Omega }^{-1}}~\text{c}{{\text{m}}^{-1}}$

(c) Semiconductors : ${{10}^{-5}}$ to ${{10}^{6}}{{\Omega }^{-1}}~\text{c}{{\text{m}}^{-1}}$

  • Conductivity of the solids may be due to the movement of electrons, holes or ions.
  • Due to presence of vacancies and other defects, solids show slight conductivity which increases with temperature.
  • Metals show electronic conductivity.
  • The conductivity of semiconductors and insulators is mainly governed by impurities and defects.

Metal oxides and sulphides have metallic to insulator behaviour at different temperatures.

concepts.title: Magnetic Properties of solids
concepts.conceptBody:
Magnetic Properties:

Based on the behavior of substances when placed in the magnetic field, these are classified into five classes and summarized in Table on next page.

concepts.title: Dielectric Properties
concepts.conceptBody:
Dielectric Properties

When a non-conducting material is placed in an electrical field, the electrons and the nuclei in the atom or molecule o that material are pulled in the opposite directions, and negative and positive charges are separated and dipoles are generated. In an electric field:

  • These dipoles amy align themselves in the same direction, so that there is net dipole moment in the crystal.
  • These dipoles may align themselves in such a manner that the net dipole moment in the crystal is zero.

Based on these facts, dielectric properties of crystals are summarised in Table 20.8 .

When any material loses its resistance for electric current, then it is called superconductor. Kammerlingh Onnes (1913) observed this phenomenon at $4~\text{K}$ in mercury. The materials offering no resistance to the flow of current at very low temperature $(2-5~\text{K})$ are called superconducting materials and phenomenon is called superconductivity.

Examples are:

(a) $\text{N}{{\text{b}}_{3}}$ Ge alloy (before 1986) (b) $\text{L}{{\text{a}}_{1.25}}\text{B}{{\text{a}}_{0.15}}\text{Cu}{{\text{O}}_{4}}(1986)$

(c) $\text{YB}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{u}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}(1987)$

Superconductors find applications in

(a) Electronics (b) building supermagnets

(c) levigation transport (d) power transmission.
